name: Alanis Harlem
quote: Heads, I press on. Tails, I press on smarter.

spheres.major: All, charm, chaos, creation, divination, healing, protection, sun, travelers

traits.name: Fortune-Favored Confidence
traits.desc: Alanis genuinely believes luck bends around them. Not arrogance — faith. When plans fail, they adapt with a grin.

traits.name: Selective Morality
traits.desc: Laws are guidelines; outcomes matter more. Stealing from the desperate is bad luck waiting to happen. Stealing from the powerful? A public service.

traits.name: Quietly Devout
traits.desc: Prayers are whispered mid-lockpick, before a leap, or while flipping a coin. Alanis distrusts grand temples and solemn sermons.

traits.name: Observant & Patient
traits.desc: Knows when to talk, when to vanish, and when to let others take the heat.

traits.name: Superstitious Rituals
traits.desc: Never enters a new city without tossing a coin at the first crossroads. Refuses to reuse a lucky die once it rolls three failures.

short: Recover a relic of Brandobaris — a coin blessed during one of his legendary thefts from the vaults of Zilchus, last seen near the Cairn Hills.
Undermine a corrupt tax collector in Dyvers who claims divine sanction from Zilchus while bleeding halfling districts dry.
long: Establish a hidden shrine-network across the Flanaess — coins left in unlikely places that subtly bless travelers and adventurers.
Pull off a legendary caper worthy of Brandobaris's attention: a theft so elegant it becomes a proverb.
Disrupt the stranglehold of Zilchus's more ruthless mercantile sects, proving that fortune should flow, not be hoarded.

adversaries.name: Sister Halvire of Zilchus
adversaries.title: Cleric of Commerce
adversaries.desc: A devout but inflexible priest who believes Brandobaris's faith undermines lawful trade. She is actively hunting 'luck-cults' under the authority of mercantile houses.

body:
I was born in the alleys where coin changes hands faster than names — near the docks of Dyvers, where merchants shout louder than priests and fortune favors the bold. My mother said I learned to walk by chasing dropped copper, and it may be true; the first prayer I ever whispered was not for mercy, but for luck.

Brandobaris heard me.

The Master of Stealth and Adventure does not call his faithful with thunder or visions. He nudges. A loose lock. A guard who looks the other way. A purse just heavy enough to matter. I learned early that risk is a form of devotion, and that cleverness is its own sacrament.

I was taken in by the Coinspinners, a loose fellowship of halflings and humans who honored Brandobaris not with temples, but with stories and shared spoils. We gathered in back rooms, cellars, and abandoned counting houses, where dice clattered like holy relics and every successful caper ended with laughter and offerings left in unlikely places. It was there I learned the twin paths of my faith: take what fate offers, and leave no trail but a grin.

Yet I am not merely a thief with a holy symbol tucked under my cloak. Brandobaris teaches that luck must flow, not stagnate. I've returned stolen gold to those ruined by chance, slipped keys to prisoners the law forgot, and turned the gears of fortune just enough to humble the powerful. A lock picked for greed dulls the blessing; a lock picked for balance sharpens it.

My talents drew attention — both welcome and dangerous. Agents of the Guild of Thieves of Greyhawk saw potential. So did a traveling priest of Olidammara, who laughed at my prayers and called Brandobaris "a sharper blade." I took that as a compliment and a warning.

Now I travel the Flanaess, drawn to ruins, secret roads, and half-forgotten vaults where chance still sleeps. I've crossed paths with smugglers along the Selintan, slipped past Iuz's patrols where even luck fears to tread, and tested my faith against traps laid by long-dead wizards who trusted steel more than fortune.

I carry Brandobaris's holy coin close to my heart, worn smooth from a thousand flips. Heads, I press on. Tails, I press on smarter.

Because luck is not blind.

It watches those bold enough to move.

And I never stand still.

name: Cirdan Anthrod
tagline: Iron Seeker · Former Border Patrol Officer of Furyondy
quote: I will not rest until the Heart is found. Not for glory. Not for reward. For honor.

traits.name: Unyielding
traits.desc: Once Cirdan commits to a course of action, he does not abandon it. Withdrawal is strategy, never surrender.

traits.name: Quietly Devout, Deeply Doubtful
traits.desc: He honors Rao in word and deed, but struggles with the god's silence after Grabford.

traits.name: Ritual of Steel
traits.desc: Cirdan cleans his weapons nightly, murmuring the names of fallen comrades as he does.

traits.name: Shield of the Helpless
traits.desc: Politics bore him; cruelty enrages him. He will always place innocents before objectives.

traits.name: Restless Sleeper
traits.desc: Sleep brings visions of crimson light, shattered earth, and a commander's final stand.

short: Learn the true fate of Sir Brennar Tormane, his former commander.
Prevent the Heart from becoming a weapon of tyranny in the hands of Iuz, Hextor, or their servants.
Redeem his failure at Grabford by ensuring the fallen did not die for nothing.
long: Recover the Heart of Aedrin — to return it to Furyondy, cleanse it of corruption, or destroy it if it has been twisted to evil.
Found a new martial order sworn to protect relics and symbols lost during the Greyhawk Wars.

adversaries.name: The Crimson Covenant
adversaries.title: War-Relic Trafficking Cult
adversaries.desc: A covert cult trafficking in war-relics and battlefield magic in Dyvers and Greyhawk City.

adversaries.name: Zhaelor the Red Hand
adversaries.title: Cambion War-Priest, Agent of Iuz
adversaries.desc: Obsessed with Furyondian relics. Zhaelor is the face of Iuz's interest in the Heart — and he has been tracking Cirdan for months.

adversaries.name: Hextorian Templars
adversaries.title: Militant Faction of Hextor
adversaries.desc: Believe the Heart of Aedrin should symbolize conquest rather than unity. They want it as a trophy and a weapon.

adversaries.name: Relic-Brokers of Greyhawk
adversaries.title: Collectors and Merchants
adversaries.desc: See sacred artifacts only as commodities. They have no ideology — only price.

body:
Cirdan Anthrod was born in the borderlands of Furyondy, near the war-scarred frontier of the Vesve Forest, where the shadow of Iuz forever looms over the north. His father was a village smith in Krestin's Ford, his mother a healer who served the small garrison stationed there. Life on the frontier taught hard lessons early — every child learned the sound of warning horns and the sickly glow of fell magic beyond the treeline.

When Cirdan came of age, he took up arms in service to King Belvor IV, joining the border patrols defending Furyondy's northern marches. He proved disciplined, relentless, and unyielding in battle, earning the nickname Iron Seeker for his refusal to abandon a charge once begun or a comrade once fallen.

During the chaos of the Greyhawk Wars, Cirdan's unit was assigned a critical duty: guarding a sacred relic known as the Heart of Aedrin. The Heart was a fist-sized crystal of crimson light, said to have been blessed by Rao during Furyondy's founding — a symbol of unity, resolve, and divine favor passed from commander to commander in times of dire need.

At a brutal engagement near Grabford, Iuzite forces unleashed profane sorcery that shattered the battlefield itself. Amid fire, screams, and collapsing earth, Cirdan watched his commander and mentor, Sir Brennar Tormane, fall mortally wounded. As the ground split open, the Heart of Aedrin vanished into the chasm along with the dead. Cirdan tried to reach it, but was dragged back as the earth swallowed everything beneath a tide of shadow and flame.

The war ended, borders shifted, and Furyondy endured — but Cirdan could not lay the past to rest. Plagued by dreams of crimson light pulsing beneath blackened soil, he resigned his commission and took to the road. Some whisper that he seeks redemption. Others claim he hunts a ghost. Cirdan knows only this: the Heart of Aedrin still exists, whether buried in the blasted lands, stolen by scavengers, or claimed by the servants of Iuz.

Now he wanders the Flanaess, selling his sword where necessary and his loyalty sparingly, following rumors wherever they lead — red light glimpsed in the ruins of Molag, whispered dealings in Greyhawk City, or cultists in Dyvers trading relics of war. He will find the Heart, even if it costs him his life, for within it lies the last unbroken symbol of Furyondy's honor — and his own.

artifact.name: The Heart of Aedrin
artifact.desc: A fist-sized crystal of crimson light, said to have been blessed by Rao himself during the founding of Furyondy. The Heart is a symbol of unity, resolve, and divine favor — passed from commander to commander in times of great trial. At Grabford, Iuzite forces unleashed profane sorcery that shattered the battlefield. As the ground split open, the Heart vanished into the chasm along with the dead.
artifact.status: Lost — vanished at Grabford · Rumored in Molag, Greyhawk City, Dyvers
artifact.warning: The Heart of Aedrin still exists. Whether buried in the blasted lands, stolen by scavengers, or claimed by the servants of Iuz.

name: Finrod Zilrich
tagline: Seeker of the Astral Codex · Chosen of Atroa
quote: I do not chase the wind. I listen — and it teaches me where to go.

traits.name: Measured Optimist
traits.desc: Finrod believes renewal is always possible, even after catastrophe. He does not deny evil — he believes it can be outgrown.

traits.name: Wind-Watcher
traits.desc: He unconsciously notes weather patterns and wind direction, often pausing mid-conversation to feel the air.

traits.name: Scholar of Restraint
traits.desc: He refuses to cast destructive magic unless no other path remains.

traits.name: Soft-Spoken Authority
traits.desc: Speaks calmly and precisely; when he raises his voice, people listen.

traits.name: Inner Conflict
traits.desc: Torn between Suel curiosity and Atroa's doctrine of renewal; fears that understanding the Rain of Colorless Fire may awaken the same arrogance.

traits.name: Superstitious Habit
traits.desc: Never opens an important book without first letting fresh air pass over its pages.

short: Locate a verified fragment or transcription related to the Astral Codex.
Study pre-Imperium Suel magic to understand what magic looked like before hubris ruled it.
Aid communities harmed by arcane misuse, especially along the Solnor Coast.
long: Prevent another arcane cataclysm like the Rain of Colorless Fire.
Decide whether the Astral Codex should be sealed, destroyed, or guarded.
Establish a small open library or tower dedicated to responsible magical study under Atroa's blessing.

adversaries.name: The Scarlet Brotherhood
adversaries.title: Suel Supremacist Faction
adversaries.desc: View Finrod as a heretic who rejects Suel destiny. Seek the Astral Codex to "restore rightful dominion."

adversaries.name: The Ashen Cabal
adversaries.title: Apocalyptic Mage Circle
adversaries.desc: A loose circle of mages who believe the Rain of Colorless Fire was necessary. Actively hunt artifacts tied to ancient apocalyptic magic.

adversaries.name: Velkyn Thrice-Bound
adversaries.title: Rival Scholar of Greyhawk City
adversaries.desc: An amoral wizard who believes Finrod lacks the will to use true power. Wants the Codex for himself — and has no hesitation about what he'd do with it.

body:
I was born in Rel Astra, where the eastern winds off the Solnor Coast carry both promise and ruin. My family name, Zilrich, once carried weight among the lesser Suel-descended houses — not greatness, but expectation. What remains now are shuttered halls, cracked towers, and spellbooks so old they smell of salt and dust. I grew up among relics of a people who believed themselves eternal, and I learned early how wrong they were.

As a child, I often climbed to the highest remaining balcony of our ancestral home to feel the wind. My mother told me Atroa rode those currents — the Lady of Spring, who carries endings away and brings beginnings in their wake. Even then, I felt the truth of it. The wind did not care for our fallen pride; it moved forward regardless. That lesson stayed with me longer than any nursery prayer.

My first spellbook was not gifted, but discovered — wedged behind a collapsed shelf, its sigils faded but alive. When I traced them, the air stirred. The breeze answered. That was the moment I knew magic was not domination, but conversation. Power did not come from command, but from understanding the rhythms of the world.

I studied under Sareth Morn, a wandering scholar who respected all gods but served none blindly. He taught me discipline and restraint, yet it was Atroa who guided my heart. Where others sought to reclaim Suel supremacy, I sought renewal — proof that even from ruin, something wiser could grow.

In the Great Library of Greyhawk, I found my purpose sharpened into obsession. Among half-crumbled scrolls and disputed marginalia, I uncovered references to the Astral Codex — a spellbook said to contain the final workings of Xodast, a Suel archmage who lived long enough to witness the Rain of Colorless Fire. The Codex is said to hold memory as much as magic — the moment of destruction, preserved like a seed locked in crystal.

That thought terrifies me.

I have followed rumors across the Cairn Hills, into the Abbor-Alz, and along the wind-scoured edges of the Bright Desert. Everywhere I go, I see the same truth repeated: civilizations fall, but the world endures. Atroa's winds still blow across shattered empires. Spring still comes.

I tell myself I seek the Codex to understand — to learn how arrogance turns knowledge into catastrophe. Yet I fear what it might awaken in me. I am Suel by blood, and no prayer can wholly cleanse that legacy. Still, Atroa teaches that stagnation is death, and that renewal requires movement, even into danger.

So I travel, staff in hand, hourglass at my belt, watching the wind and listening for its guidance. If I find the Astral Codex, I will not use it to reclaim what was lost. I will use it to ensure that when the world stands on the brink again, someone remembers how easily fire can fall from the sky.

And when the east wind rises, I follow.

artifact.name: The Astral Codex
artifact.desc: A spellbook said to contain the final workings of Xodast, a Suel archmage who lived long enough to witness the Rain of Colorless Fire. The Codex is said to hold memory as much as magic — the moment of destruction, preserved like a seed locked in crystal.
artifact.status: Sought — last rumored near the Cairn Hills

name: Feanor of Dreadwood
tagline: Warden of the Dreadwood · Shadow's Equal
quote: In the Dreadwood, the wind does not forget. Neither do I.

traits.name: Quietly Observant
traits.desc: Speaks little, but notices everything — changes in the wind, nervous glances, footprints where none should be.

traits.name: Measured Precision
traits.desc: Dislikes waste, whether of words, arrows, or lives. Every shot has intent.

traits.name: Warden's Instinct
traits.desc: Instinctively places himself between danger and others, even when it costs him.

traits.name: Elven Reserve
traits.desc: Polite but distant with most humans; more at ease in the wilds or among elves.

traits.name: Unyielding Focus
traits.desc: Once he sets his sights on a target — be it prey, truth, or enemy — he does not abandon it.

traits.name: Hidden Curiosity
traits.desc: Despite his stoic exterior, deeply curious about ancient elven lore and forgotten magic, especially anything tied to Celene or elemental corruption.

short: Identify the midnight-feathered archer haunting the wilds around Saltmarsh.
Prevent smugglers and cultists from using the Dreadwood and Hool Marshes as hidden routes.
Discover who is marking the land with the Eye in the Flame and why.
long: Expose and destroy any resurgence of the Temple of Elemental Evil or its splinter cults.
Uncover his mother's true past in Celene, and why she fled.
Establish a permanent ranger watch over the Dreadwood to protect Saltmarsh.
One day craft or earn a legendary bow worthy of elven tradition — possibly one tied to ancient Greyhawk lore.

adversaries.name: The Midnight Archer
adversaries.title: Unknown Rival
adversaries.desc: A master bowman using midnight-blue fletching. Their motives are unclear — sometimes opposing smugglers, sometimes interfering with patrols. They may be testing him... or grooming him for something darker.

adversaries.name: Elemental Cult Remnants
adversaries.title: Temple of Elemental Evil Cells
adversaries.desc: Hidden cells operating along the coast and marshes. They mark territory with subtle sigils — the Eye in the Flame — and use hired muscle when secrecy fails.

adversaries.name: Saltmarsh Smuggler Rings
adversaries.title: Criminal Networks
adversaries.desc: Running illicit goods through the marshes and forest paths. He has ruined shipments — and livelihoods. They remember.

adversaries.name: Lizardfolk Scouts of Dunwater
adversaries.title: Wary Neighbors
adversaries.desc: Not openly hostile, but deeply suspicious of surface folk. Some see him as a recurring threat to their territory.

adversaries.name: An Elven Agent of Celene
adversaries.title: Political Operative
adversaries.desc: A quiet watcher, uncertain whether Feanor is an asset or a liability tied to his mother's past. Has not yet acted.

body:
I was born beneath the boughs of the Dreadwood, though my blood runs pure from the high towers of Celene. My mother left that distant elven kingdom long before I was born — she sought a quieter life, or so she said. She never spoke much about why she truly fled Celene's courts, but I learned early that even the most graceful folk can carry shadows in their hearts. She raised me among the wardens of the Dreadwood, teaching me the art of the bow before I could steady my own breath.

My father was a man of Saltmarsh, a hunter who sold furs and smoked fish by the docks. He had no elven grace, no ancient wisdom, but he taught me how to survive among people — to judge a man by his hands and his eyes, not his words. Between him and my mother, I learned to move like a whisper and to shoot like a striking hawk.

I joined the forest wardens as soon as I was old enough. The Dreadwood is a place that swallows the unprepared — lizardfolk raiders, smugglers, and marsh-creatures test its borders daily. I thought I understood its dangers. I was wrong.

One morning, I followed a trail of broken branches to a clearing where I found arrows — fine arrows — embedded in the old oaks. Long, elegant shafts of unfamiliar design. Each fletched with midnight-blue feathers. They were placed deliberately, almost like a challenge. And their marksmanship... they weren't meant to kill. They were meant to demonstrate skill. A message, aimed at me.

Not long after, the rumors began. A ghostly archer striking from the shadows along the coast, driving off wardens, cutting smuggler lines, and sometimes leaving messages carved into driftwood. Some said it was an outlaw ranger. Others whispered it was a renegade elf from Celene — one with a grudge. My mother grew quiet when I asked her, and that silence told me more than any story.

I've begun finding those midnight-feathered arrows closer to Saltmarsh now — near the mines, the marsh paths, even outside my father's old hut. Someone is hunting me. Or testing me. Or both.

Meanwhile, darker things stir. I discovered the Eye in the Flame symbol — the same sigil tied to the old Temple of Elemental Evil — burned into a tree along the Dunwater River. Whatever force once threatened the world is waking, whispering from the marshes.

So now I walk between Saltmarsh and the wilds, bow always strung. People trust me enough to scout the borders, though their eyes linger too long on my pointed ears and my silence. I do not seek glory. I seek answers — about the cult rising again, and about the unseen archer whose skill rivals my own, whose arrows seem to guide me toward something inevitable.

If the darkness returns, I will face it. And if my phantom rival steps from the shadows, bow in hand, I will face them too. Whether they are meant to be my enemy... or my teacher.

Either way, someone must walk the quiet paths and guard Saltmarsh from what creeps toward it.

And it seems that someone is me.
roleplay_cue: When tense, he unconsciously checks his bowstring or touches the fletching of his arrows.

name: Thalion Sunholme
quote: Saltmarsh may not be a fortress, but it is my home. And no darkness will claim it while I stand.

traits.name: Steady Presence
traits.desc: Speaks little, but when he does, people listen. He weighs words as carefully as sword blows.

traits.name: Unshakeable Resolve
traits.desc: Once he commits to a course of action, fear, threats, or temptation rarely sway him.

traits.name: Protective Instinct
traits.desc: Instinctively positions himself between danger and others — physically and socially.

traits.name: Suspicious of Authority
traits.desc: The wars taught him that banners and titles do not equal virtue.

traits.name: Quiet Compassion
traits.desc: Helps without being seen — repairing nets, standing night watch, escorting the vulnerable.

traits.name: Burdened by Memory
traits.desc: Carries the weight of those he failed to save during the chaos after the wars.

short: Investigate the abandoned house near Saltmarsh, determining whether it is truly haunted or a front for criminal activity.
Break the grip of smugglers and raiders exploiting the marshes and coastline.
Restore confidence among Saltmarsh's folk so fear no longer rules their lives.
long: Establish a defensive brotherhood or watch in Saltmarsh — sworn to a code rather than a crown or church.
Prevent the Wild Coast from descending into the lawlessness that followed the Greyhawk Wars.
Prove that honor can survive even when the old orders and faiths have failed.

adversaries.name: Smugglers of the Azure Coast
adversaries.title: Criminal Network
adversaries.desc: View Thalion as a growing threat to their operations and profits. His presence in Saltmarsh has already disrupted several runs.

adversaries.name: Marsh Raiders
adversaries.title: Opportunist Warbands
adversaries.desc: Brutal opportunists who believe fear is the only law that matters. They have learned his name.

body:
Saltmarsh was never a place for heroes. It's a town that clings to life between the hungry sea and the schemes of those who'd rather see it swallowed. After the Greyhawk Wars, when the world seemed to sag under the weight of its own scars, Saltmarsh changed too — trade dried up, fear took root, and the people learned to keep their heads down.

I refused to do the same.

I wasn't born to any noble order or sacred calling. No shining sign struck me from the heavens. My oath grew from something simpler — and perhaps stronger: seeing too much suffering and deciding I would no longer stand aside. The wars had left a wake of bandits, slavers, deserters, and worse along the Wild Coast and the Azure frontier. Saltmarsh was small, but it was vulnerable, and danger sees opportunity in places like this.

I took up the blade when I was old enough to lift it, and the code I forged for myself became my compass.

Some call me a paladin. I don't argue the term, though mine is a different sort than the old tales describe. My power doesn't come from a god's blessing — it comes from resolve, discipline, and a will hardened by everything I've seen.

Saltmarsh tested that resolve more than once. The town is rebuilding — slowly, painfully — and enemies circle like sharks in shallow water. Smugglers use the marshes as their sanctuary. Raiders strike from the sea. And recently, whispers have begun anew about strange goings-on at the abandoned house outside town. Some say it's haunted. Others say it's a hideout for criminals. I don't much care which — both need confronting.

When I walk the streets at dusk and see lantern light flicker in the windows, I'm reminded why I hold fast to my oath. This town is worth protecting. These people deserve a chance to live without fear, even in these broken years after the wars.

Saltmarsh may not be a fortress, but it is my home. And as long as I draw breath, no darkness — ghostly or mortal — will claim it without facing me first.
oath: Stand between danger and the innocent.
Speak truth, even when silence is safer.
Act with honor, even when victory tempts otherwise.
And never allow fear to rule the hearts of good folk.
roleplay_cue: He maintains his weapons meticulously, especially before entering old ruins or abandoned buildings — order imposed on decay.

deityCompass.description: Thalion swore no oath to any god. He forged his own code from what he has seen — what was lost, what was abandoned, what still needed protecting. The gods, if they are watching, have not yet spoken. But actions leave marks. And something is listening.

deities.name: Heironeous
deities.description: The god of knights and champions. He calls those who fight not for glory, but because someone must stand between evil and the world.

deities.name: Mayaheine
deities.domain: Protection, Justice, Valor for the Defenseless
deities.description: A young goddess, herself once mortal. She calls those who stand before the helpless not because they were born to it — but because they chose to.

deities.name: Pholtus
deities.description: The god of those who do not bend. His light is not warm — it is clarifying. He favors the certain, the unyielding, those who hold the line when others break.

deities.name: Rao
deities.description: The god of calm in chaos. He does not demand warriors — he calls those who resolve what others would simply cut through.

deities.name: Fortubo
deities.description: Old god of dwarves and defenders. He favors those who dig in and do not move — not because they cannot flee, but because they have decided this ground matters.

deities.name: Pelor
deities.description: The god of warmth and renewal. He favors those who heal before they strike — and strike only so others may heal.

deities.name: Zodal
deities.description: The god of those who forgive when they have every reason not to. He watches for acts of grace that cost something.

deities.name: Berei
deities.description: The goddess of the hearth and the harvest. She does not ask for champions — she asks for neighbors. Those who quietly keep others alive.

deities.name: Fharlanghn
deities.domain: Roads, Travel, Distance, the Long Watch
deities.description: The god of those who keep moving — not to flee, but because the road itself is the calling. He watches those who walk toward trouble when others walk away.

deities.name: Trithereon
deities.description: The god of those who will not be owned. He watches those who refuse to let fear govern — not because law demands it, but because they simply will not bend.

deities.name: Kord
deities.description: The god of those who throw themselves in without guarantee of winning. He has no patience for careful plans — only for the moment you step forward anyway.

deities.name: St. Cuthbert
deities.description: The god of stubborn righteousness. He has no patience for ceremony — only for doing what is right, bluntly and completely.

deities.name: Osprem
deities.description: Goddess of those who brave the water to protect others. She honors those who go out in the storm to bring others home.

deities.name: Obad-Hai
deities.description: The god of the natural world, indifferent to morality but not to truth. He watches those who act without needing an audience.

deities.name: Celestian
deities.description: The god of those who look upward and feel something wordless. He favors the patient observer — those who see the shape of things before acting.

deities.name: Hextor
deities.description: The dark twin of Heironeous. He watches those whose love of order tips toward control — whose protection becomes possession. He is patient.

deities.name: Erythnul
deities.description: Not a god to be sought. But those who let anger become habit, who stop asking why they fight — sometimes they find he was already watching.

deities.name: Nerull
deities.description: The harvester of the unredeemed dead. He makes no claim until the moment comes. But some who begin with purpose end with him.

journal.event: Spared a goblin during the haunted house ambush
journal.note: The party killed most of them. When it was over, one remained. He let it go.

name: Zaphod Beeblebrox
tagline: Gnomish Professor of the Resonant Arts
quote: A song is a key. Knowledge is the door. Sing well, and all may enter.

traits.name: Brilliant, Irreverent & Verbose
traits.desc: Zaphod is distractingly learned and impossible to shut up. Every observation becomes a lecture, every lecture a performance.

traits.name: Humor as Shield and Scalpel
traits.desc: Uses wit as both armor and weapon — a well-placed joke can defuse a crowd or gut an argument. He knows the difference.

traits.name: Genuinely Cares
traits.desc: For students, common folk, and the forgotten alike. Not performative — he will go out of his way for people who remind him why truth matters.

traits.name: Principled Fury
traits.desc: Hates bullies, despots, and sloppy footnotes with equal passion. The first two he fights with songs. The third he corrects in red ink.

long: Undermine Iuz's myth of godhood through music, satire, and counter-resonance.
Preserve forbidden histories through song before they are erased entirely.
Train a new generation of bards as scholars and truth-bearers.

adversaries.name: Iuz the Old
adversaries.title: Demigod of Pain and Oppression
adversaries.desc: Iuz's cultists burned Grey College's music wing and issued a standing order: "Silence the gnome." Zaphod responded by singing louder.

body:
Zaphod Beeblebrox was born beneath the low, root-tangled hills of the Kron Hills, where gnomes value cleverness, song, and stories that teach as much as they entertain. From an early age, Zaphod showed an uncommon talent — not merely for music, but for understanding why music works. He could explain how a marching rhythm stiffened a spine, how a minor key seeded dread, and how a well-placed pause could make a lie unravel itself.

Rather than becoming a simple minstrel, Zaphod pursued scholarship. He traveled to Grey College in the Free City of Greyhawk, where he studied history, rhetoric, arcane theory, and the obscure discipline he would later name Resonant Pedagogy — the use of song, poetry, and performance to transmit truth, memory, and resistance. In time, he became a professor, a rarity among bards, lecturing in packed halls while standing atop desks so all could see him gesticulate wildly with pipe, lute, and chalk.

Zaphod's enmity with Iuz the Old began not on a battlefield, but in an archive.

While cataloging pre-Greyhawk Wars folk songs, Zaphod uncovered a suppressed cycle of ballads from the Howling Hills, coded with mnemonic harmonies. When performed correctly, the verses revealed firsthand accounts of Iuz's early atrocities — names of villages erased, demons bound through betrayal, and proof that Iuz's divinity was neither inevitable nor invincible. Worse still, the songs contained counter-resonances — melodic structures that weakened fear-based enchantments and disrupted certain demonic compulsions.

Zaphod published his findings in a controversial treatise: "The Tyrant's Chorus: A Musical Analysis of Iuzian Fear-Myth."

The reaction was immediate. Cultists attempted to burn Grey College's music wing. Students vanished. A horned emissary of Iuz delivered a single message etched into a lectern: "Silence the gnome." Instead, Zaphod sang louder.

Forced into exile, Zaphod now wanders the Flanaess, posing as a traveling lecturer, tavern performer, or eccentric storyteller. Wherever Iuz's influence spreads, Zaphod follows — teaching coded songs to rebels, embedding historical truths into drinking chants, and using satire to puncture the illusion of Iuz's omnipotence. His performances often seem comedic and chaotic, but every rhyme is deliberate, every refrain a weapon.

Zaphod believes tyranny survives only so long as people forget how it began. His life's work is to ensure they remember.

treatise.title: The Tyrant's Chorus: A Musical Analysis of Iuzian Fear-Myth
treatise.byline: Professor Zaphod Beeblebrox, Grey College (displaced), Chair Emeritus of Resonant Pedagogy
treatise.excerpt:
Let us begin by correcting a common and dangerous misconception: that Iuz is feared because he is powerful. The inverse is nearer the truth. Iuz is powerful because he is feared — and fear, like any poorly taught song, can be unlearned.

The priests of Iuz insist that terror is his natural emanation, as heat is to flame. This is false. Fear is composed. It has meter, repetition, and refrain. It relies upon predictable cadences: the whispered rumor, the exaggerated reprisal, the ritualized cruelty performed just loudly enough to be overheard. These are not acts of divinity. They are performances.

In my analysis of the Howling Hills ballads (Appendix III, verses 12–19), we find a recurring harmonic anomaly: a countermelody hummed beneath the work-songs of the enslaved. When sung alone, it is unremarkable. When sung together — particularly in thirds — it produces a measurable disruption in compulsion effects commonly attributed to demonic influence. The singers did not know why it worked. They only knew that overseers grew uneasy when it was sung.

This is the tyrant's great weakness: fear demands silence from its victims, but music thrives on breath.

Consider the so-called "divine wrath" of Iuz. Note how often it arrives preceded by proclamation, sigil, or sermon. True divinity needs no herald. Iuz must announce himself because his power depends upon anticipation. Remove the audience, and the god diminishes to a bully shouting in an empty hall.

I have personally demonstrated — at regrettable personal cost — that satirical verse aimed at Iuz's origin myths provokes a response disproportionate to its alleged insignificance. Why should a god fear a joke? Because humor breaks rhythm. It causes the chorus to falter.

Therefore, I submit this thesis: Iuz is not sustained by worship, but by participation. Those who repeat his stories, obey his rituals, or whisper his threats are unwitting choristers in his song.

To resist him, one must not merely fight — but sing differently.

Teach the children the old tunes with new words. Hide history in drinking songs. Mock the tyrant's cadence until it can no longer march. When enough voices refuse the refrain, the chorus collapses.

And when that day comes, Iuz will learn the final lesson of all false gods:

No song survives when no one remembers how it goes.

— Z. Beeblebrox, written in exile, with excessive footnotes and insufficient safety precautions
